title: p-Curvature and Non-Abelian Cohomology
speaker: Daniel Litt
abstract:
The cohomology of a family of algebraic varieties carries a number of interrelated structures, of both Hodge-theoretic and arithmetic flavors. I’ll explain joint work with Josh Lam developing analogues of some of these structures and interrelations for nonabelian cohomology—that is, the space of representations of the fundamental groups of a family of algebraic varieties, in its various incarnations. As an application, I’ll explain a non-abelian version of Katz’s 1972 proof of the p-curvature conjecture for Gauss-Manin connections. Namely, let X→S
 be a smooth proper morphism. We show that if the p-curvature of the isomonodromy foliation on the moduli space of flat bundles on X/S
 vanishes, then the action of π1(S,s) on the space of conjugacy classes of representations of π1(Xs) into GLn(Z) factors through a finite group.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Abelian p-curvature template
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a smooth proper family \(X\to S\) and reviews the abelian story: cohomology as a local system, its de Rham realization with Gauss--Manin connection, p-curvature in characteristic \(p\), the Grothendieck--Katz conjecture, and Katz's theorem for Gauss--Manin connections.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian cohomology and isomonodromy
proof_stages.summary: The abelian monodromy representation is replaced by the action of \(\pi_1(S)\) on Betti moduli of representations of \(\pi_1\) of the fiber, while the de Rham realization is replaced by the moduli of flat bundles equipped with the algebraic isomonodromy foliation.

proof_stages.label: Foliation p-closedness conjecture and main theorems
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the Esnault--Shepherd-Barron--Taylor type conjecture that p-closed foliations have algebraic leaves, the speaker states joint theorems with Josh Lam giving finite action on integral Betti points and algebraicity of isomonodromy leaves on components containing a \(\overline{\mathbb Z}\)-point, in particular the trivial component.

proof_stages.label: Katz proof strategy to imitate
proof_stages.summary: Katz's proof is organized into three logical steps: an arithmetic Katz formula relating p-curvature to preservation of the Hodge filtration, a geometric Hodge-index step giving unitary monodromy, and a topological compact-discrete argument yielding finiteness.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian Hodge filtration and Katz formula
proof_stages.summary: Simpson's Hodge moduli space of \(\lambda\)-connections is used as the nonabelian Hodge filtration, and the arithmetic theorem A/nonabelian Katz formula says that p-closedness of the isomonodromy foliation for infinitely many primes forces this foliation to extend across \(\lambda=0\).

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian Hodge-index consequences
proof_stages.summary: The geometric replacement for the Hodge index theorem shows that preservation of the nonabelian Hodge filtration gives compact closures of Betti orbits and, on the smooth locus, preservation of a Kähler metric via the energy functional.

proof_stages.label: Integral discreteness and finiteness of the action
proof_stages.summary: Compactness of Betti orbits is combined with discreteness of integral points to prove finite orbits and, after uniformity input from arithmetic dynamics, theorem one; theorem two is then obtained from finite orbits, unitary integral tangent actions, and rigidity of Riemannian isometries.

examples.name: Algebraic curves with many integral Betti points
examples.note: In response to a question about replacing \(\mathbb Z\) by rings of integers, curves are cited as familiar varieties whose character varieties often have many integral points, illustrating why the finite-action theorem is meaningful despite possible nonemptiness issues.
examples.engagement_reason: Curves are invoked as familiar varieties with many integral/Z points (representations with integral traces), but no property is checked or construction carried out on a particular curve.

passes.reason: Algebraic curves are used as familiar varieties whose Betti moduli have many integral points, not as sporadic, degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or refuting cases.

examples.name: Rank-two Betti-moduli components
examples.note: The case \(r=2\) is mentioned as a special situation, using work of Corlette--Simpson, where all components have the needed \(\overline{\mathbb Z}\)-point and hence the componentwise algebraicity theorem applies to every component.
examples.engagement_reason: The rank-two case is cited as one where the result holds for all Betti-moduli components via known work, without working through any rank-two component concretely.

passes.reason: Rank two is the minimal genuinely non-abelian rank for these Betti-moduli considerations, and is cited as a positive low-rank case where all components are covered, not as a counterexample.
Rank two is the minimal non-abelian rank case for these Betti moduli spaces, used as a positive case where all components are covered.
Rank-two Betti-moduli components are a standard low-rank positive case where the result holds for all components, not a structural exception or counterexample.
Rank-two Betti-moduli components are a low-rank standard family case used to indicate the theorem’s scope, not a sporadic, degenerate, symmetric, or adversarial example.
